title: AI, Productivity & the Bullish Medium-Term Outlook

stocks on a record-breaking rally.

could end up hiking interest rates next
year. We begin this hour with stocks
holding at record highs as optimism
grows over an end to the war. Darius

12-month outlook is bullish. The next
few months, however, may prove volatile.
Darius joins us now for more. Darius,
good morning. Good to see you. Oh, great
to see you, John. Thanks for
>> to the program, as well. What's going to
be the source of that volatility? Well,
I think we're we're not out of the woods
yet as it relates to the Strait of
Hormuz dynamic. And we also have the
return of crowded bull's positioning
across global financial markets. So, if
we get another Axios headline that, you
know, is leaking in the wrong direction,
then you can have a little bit more
volatility. But when you take a
medium-term view on the markets and on
the economy, things are about as good as
they've been in a really long time. I'd
say since early 2021.
>> Let's just stay on that. Look at the
economic data. Look at the earnings. How
impressive is it that we've got a
central bank that's still holding on to
an easing bias?
Well, that's that's a totally separate
conversation. But the thing I'd say
about the Fed is the biggest mistake the
Fed can make this year is doing
anything. This is economy that has
accelerating inflation. When you look at
the good inflation components that they
care about, this economy that is
accelerating bank credit growth. At the
same time, we have a productivity boom.
And we are In our view, we think we're
heading into a jobless recovery. So, on
the the push-pull dynamic there, I
should say the Fed should stay on hold.
I think they would be The markets would
probably not like the Fed doing anything
in any one direction because it would
create tension on the other side of the
mandate. It's not totally unrelated,
though. The whole point is when you have
such robust growth, to John's point,
strongest as you were saying since 2021,
and then you have a Fed with an easing
bias, how much does that increase the
risk of an inflationary kind of impulse
that it creates a headwind that people
aren't really pricing in? Yeah, well, I
think we're already seeing the
inflationary impulse. If you look at,
for instance, core PCE inflation on a
3-month annualized basis, we're at 4.4%.
Super core PCE inflation, we're at 4.5%.
So, we already have the inflationary
impulse. And so, the best thing the Fed
can do right now is to jawbone that away
to this to the extent possible. In our
view, we think it's going to go away
because if you look at the relationship
between productivity growth, we think
we're in a 150 to 200 basis point
acceleration in trend productivity
growth. That should knock off about 40
to 50 basis points from trend inflation.
While at the same time, you have some
very depressing components in the labor
market that are weighing on wage
inflation from a cyclical and structural
standpoint. The the real inflation
dynamic in the economy, when we get past
this inflation shock, should be much
lower in our opinion. You said that
there was this depressive factor coming
from the labor market. How would this
market treat a very hot labor market
report tomorrow? Let's say the number of
jobs created much above what people had
expected. Let's say wage increases much
more than people expected. Yeah, I think
what's going to happen is the market's
going to price in more tightening in the
short rate short rates curve. And so,
that's the my opinion I think that would
be
an appropriate response from the market.
But the reality is we don't think that
dynamic is sustainable. When you look
at, you know, for instance, core private
services payrolls, which lops off
healthcare, social services, education,
and government, basically everything
that the government is the main consumer
or the employer of, that peaked two
years ago.
Two years ago. And so, in our view, we
think this AI dynamic is real. I mean,
it's hard to to statistically test it,
but we think it's real. We think we've
never seen a trend like this with the
booming economy.
>> You think when it comes to AI that
actually it's going to help
international stocks over US stocks?
Yeah, I think it's going to help all the
stocks that have been left behind by the
mega cap tech companies. And the reason
we say that is not because we're bearish
on the mega cap tech companies, it's
that we think that the transfer of that
technology
will [clears throat] create a
convergence in productivity, create a
convergence in margins, create a
convergence in ultimately in valuations
with the stocks that have been left
behind from a capital allocation
standpoint. When will we see that?
I think it's all it's already ongoing.
The trade started a year and a half ago.
So, I think that trade, I mean, we
obviously took a pause in the context of
this, you know, Strait of Hormuz dynamic
because that's a cyclical headwind in
the economy. Once we open the Strait of
Hormuz and those barrels of petroleum
products are flowing, I think the
market's going to start to look around
and say, "Hey, we have a lot of crowded
bullish positioning here. We We don't
have any crowded bullish positioning
here, but we're going to see a
convergence in the in the operating
net."
>> Just sit on that just for a moment. The
biggest risk to the tech rally might be
a reopening of the street. Yeah, well,
on a relative basis. I don't think
you're going to sell tech stocks on
that, but I think on a relative basis,
yeah. Can we just finish on the capex
boom from big tech? Do you think that is
a bubble? How do you think about the
capex intentions from a handful of
companies? Well, look, I
I'm not smart enough to determine if it
is or is not a bubble, but I will say
very better much better investors than I
have have said when something doubles in
3 years and we obviously seeing it
double in the AI capex in 1 year, it's a
bubble. And so, the the key takeaway is
that whenever you have these historical
capex bubbles, you go back to the
railroad bubble, the consumer durable
goods bubble, the and and the IT capex
bubble, they tend to end in secular bear
markets. So, I think that's where we're
headed longer term, but I don't think
that's a medium-term risk. What do you
do with chips in the meantime? It seems
to be a question I get a lot within this
massive rally. Something that stuck out
for me this week was a comment from
Barclays. Mhm. We caught up with Mr.
Altman over there and he said, "We need
to focus on where we are, not how we got
here. How we got here was super fast,
vicious rally. Where are we now? The
valuation, what is the price we're
paying now for somebody's chip stories?"
Yeah, I mean, it's Looney Tunes price,
but again, at twice as silly price is
not twice as silly, right? I think our
friend David Einhorn said that a while
back in a meeting. So,
the thing I'd just say on the chip
stocks is that that that that you you
have to take profits and start to look
at businesses that are going to have a
faster convergence in their in their
profitability. These are lower
comparative base businesses from the
perspective of margins and from the
perspective of earnings. So, any you
transfer that technology, we're going to
have a faster rate of change in their
growth rates and that in our opinion,
that's a durable catalyst.
